ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for sambal maut or raw chilli padi condiment. FOODON_03301710 (chili pepper food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Chilli peppers and products thereof) is the closest parent. No specific sambal maut entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to sambal maut or raw chilli padi condiment. SNOMED CT coverage of Southeast Asian condiments is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sambal maut as a condiment is approximately 10 g (roughly one teaspoon), served alongside a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Peppers, hot chili, red, raw (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FDC ID 170108)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sambal maut as described is freshly served raw chilli padi (bird's eye chilli / Capsicum frutescens). No direct entry for sambal maut or chilli padi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at the required specificity. USDA SR Legacy entry for raw hot red chilli pepper (FDC ID 170108) is used as the closest defensible analogue for raw chilli padi. Values are adapted from this entry. Bird's eye chilli is nutritionally comparable to raw hot red chilli pepper per FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association; GI values cross-referenced with University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sambal maut or chilli padi. Raw chilli peppers are a non-starchy vegetable with very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic impact. A GI of approximately 15 is assigned by analogy with other raw non-starchy vegetables and chilli pepper products documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 10 g serving = (15 × 0.88 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~9 mg per 100 g); capsaicin may have mild vasodilatory effects
hypertension.guidance: Sambal maut is very low in sodium and poses minimal hypertension risk; however, individuals with severe hypertension should monitor total condiment intake if salt is added during preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15) and negligible glycemic load per serving; capsaicin may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of 0.1 per 10 g serving, sambal maut has negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is generally safe for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as a condiment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.04 g per 100 g); capsaicin associated with anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties in peer-reviewed literature
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sambal maut is extremely low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free; regular moderate consumption of chilli-based condiments has been associated with favourable cardiovascular outcomes in observational studies, though portion control is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sambal Maut, literally meaning 'deadly sambal' in Malay and Indonesian, is an intensely spicy raw chilli condiment made primarily from chilli padi (bird's eye chilli, Capsicum frutescens). It is served fresh and uncooked, preserving the full heat and pungency of the chillies. The dish is a staple condiment in Indonesian and Malay cuisine, commonly served alongside rice dishes, martabak, grilled meats, and noodles. The flavour profile is fiercely hot, with a sharp, fresh, slightly fruity heat characteristic of bird's eye chillies. Nutritionally, raw chilli padi is low in calories (~40 kcal per 100 g), very low in fat, and provides meaningful amounts of vitamin C (approximately 144 mg per 100 g), vitamin A, potassium, and dietary fibre. Its glycemic index is very low (~15), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Capsaicin, the active compound responsible for the heat, has been studied for potential anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and metabolic benefits. Clinically, sambal maut is low in sodium and saturated fat, posing minimal concern for hypertension or cardiovascular disease when consumed as a condiment. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. In Singapore, it is offered at Indonesian restaurants and food stalls, often as an optional spice-level condiment.
common_user_queries: What is sambal maut made of?
Is sambal maut suitable for diabetics?
What does sambal maut taste like?

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for sambal maut or chilli padi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD at the required specificity. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw hot red chilli pepper (FDC ID 170108), which is the closest defensible analogue. GI is estimated by analogy with non-starchy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170108 (Peppers, hot chili, red, raw) as the closest approved-source analogue for raw chilli padi. FAO/INFOODS SEA data supports comparability of bird's eye chilli to raw hot red chilli pepper. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; raw chilli peppers contain no trans fats by nature (naturally zero). 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based food with no animal-derived components. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; raw chilli peppers contain no meaningful vitamin D. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero for a plant-based food. 6. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate for raw non-starchy chilli pepper; no direct GI measurement for sambal maut or chilli padi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170108. 8. This record represents the raw chilli padi component only; if sambal maut includes additional ingredients such as shrimp paste (belacan), garlic, or lime juice, sodium and other values may differ. 9. The food is classified as Indonesian cuisine; mandatory Indonesian local name is satisfied by 'Sambal Maut'. 10. No -1 sentinel values remain undocumented; all -1 fields have been replaced with sourced numeric values from USDA SR Legacy where available.
